title: Comparison Shopping & Impulse Control
summary: Simple habits that quietly save you hundreds of dollars a year.
doThisToday: Pick one subscription or app purchase you've made recently and check if there was a cheaper way to get the same thing.

cards.prompt: The same item is almost never the same price everywhere.
cards.explanation: Checking two or three places before buying something over $20 is one of the highest-value habits you can build — a few minutes of comparing can easily save 10-30%.

cards.prompt: The same sneakers are $80 at one store and $65 at another, but the $65 store charges $12 shipping and has a no-returns policy. Which is the better deal?
options: Always the lower listed price, no exceptions
It depends — once you add shipping and factor in the return policy, the $80 option might actually be cheaper or safer overall
Shipping and return policy never matter
cards.explanation: The listed price is never the whole story. Shipping, taxes, and return policies can flip which option is actually cheaper — or worth the extra few dollars for the safety net.

cards.prompt: True or false: the cheapest price you can find is always the smartest choice.
cards.explanation: Sometimes the cheapest option comes with worse quality, no returns, or hidden fees. "Cheapest" and "best deal" aren't always the same thing.

cards.prompt: Now for impulse control — because comparison shopping doesn't help if you buy before you even compare.
cards.explanation: One simple trick: the 24-hour rule. For any non-essential purchase over some amount you set (say $30), wait 24 hours before buying. If you still want it tomorrow, it's probably a real want, not just a scroll-triggered urge.

cards.prompt: You see a $70 jacket on Instagram at 11pm and almost buy it immediately. Using the 24-hour rule, what should you do?
options: Buy it now before the price changes
Add it to your cart or save the link, then decide tomorrow with a clear head
Never buy anything you see on social media, ever
cards.explanation: The rule isn't "never buy things you see online" — it's giving your brain time to separate a real want from a late-night impulse. If it still feels worth it tomorrow, go for it.

cards.prompt: A shirt costs $24. Shipping is $6. Your state sales tax is 8% on the $24 item price. What's the total cost, rounded to the nearest dollar?
cards.explanation: $24 × 1.08 = $25.92 for the item with tax, plus $6 shipping = $31.92, which rounds to $32. That's 33% more than the sticker price — exactly why checking the real total matters.

cards.prompt: Put the smart-shopping checklist in the right order, from first step to last.

steps.label: Add up shipping and tax for the real total
Check reviews and the return policy
Wait 24 hours if it's not essential

cards.explanation: Each step catches something the one before it misses — comparing catches a bad price, the real total catches hidden fees, reviews catch quality issues, and the wait catches pure impulse.

cards.prompt: Two habits, huge payoff: compare before you buy, and give yourself 24 hours on anything that isn't essential.
cards.explanation: Neither one requires willpower in the moment — they're just small systems that do the hard work for you.
